([/ˈlæmdə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-lambda.ogg "File:En-us-lambda.ogg");[[1]](#cite_note-OEDPron-1) uppercase **Λ**, lowercase **λ**; [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): λάμ(β)δα, *lám(b)da*; [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): λά(μ)βδα, *lá(m)bda*), sometimes rendered **lamda**,[[2]](#cite_note-OEDForms-2) **labda**[[2]](#cite_note-OEDForms-2) or **lamma**,[[2]](#cite_note-OEDForms-2) is the eleventh letter of the [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet"), representing the [voiced alveolar lateral approximant](/wiki/Dental,_alveolar_and_postalveolar_lateral_approximants "Dental, alveolar and postalveolar lateral approximants") IPA: [[l]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek"); it derives from the [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenician_alphabet "Phoenician alphabet") letter [Lamed](/wiki/Lamed "Lamed"), and gave rise to [Latin](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") [L](/wiki/L "L") and [Cyrillic](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") [El](/wiki/El_(Cyrillic) "El (Cyrillic)") (Л). In the system of [Greek numerals](/wiki/Greek_numerals "Greek numerals"), lambda has a value of 30. The ancient [grammarians](/wiki/Alexandrine_grammarians "Alexandrine grammarians") typically called it λάβδα (*lắbdă*, [[lábda]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")) in [Classical Greek](/wiki/Classical_Greek "Classical Greek") times,[[3]](#cite_note-3) whereas in [Modern Greek](/wiki/Modern_Greek "Modern Greek") it is λάμδα (*lámda*, [[ˈlamða]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")), while the spelling λάμβδα (*lámbda*) was used (to varying degrees) throughout the lengthy transition between the two.

**Lambda** ([/ˈlæmdə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-lambda.ogg "File:En-us-lambda.ogg");[[1]](#cite_note-OEDPron-1) uppercase **Λ**, lowercase **λ**; [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): λάμ(β)δα, *lám(b)da*; [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): λά(μ)βδα, *lá(m)bda*), sometimes rendered **lamda**,[[2]](#cite_note-OEDForms-2) **labda**[[2]](#cite_note-OEDForms-2) or **lamma**,[[2]](#cite_note-OEDForms-2) is the eleventh letter of the [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet"), representing the [voiced alveolar lateral approximant](/wiki/Dental,_alveolar_and_postalveolar_lateral_approximants "Dental, alveolar and postalveolar lateral approximants") IPA: [[l]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek"); it derives from the [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenician_alphabet "Phoenician alphabet") letter [Lamed](/wiki/Lamed "Lamed"), and gave rise to [Latin](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") [L](/wiki/L "L") and [Cyrillic](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") [El](/wiki/El_(Cyrillic) "El (Cyrillic)") (Л). In the system of [Greek numerals](/wiki/Greek_numerals "Greek numerals"), lambda has a value of 30. The ancient [grammarians](/wiki/Alexandrine_grammarians "Alexandrine grammarians") typically called it λάβδα (*lắbdă*, [[lábda]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")) in [Classical Greek](/wiki/Classical_Greek "Classical Greek") times,[[3]](#cite_note-3) whereas in [Modern Greek](/wiki/Modern_Greek "Modern Greek") it is λάμδα (*lámda*, [[ˈlamða]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")), while the spelling λάμβδα (*lámbda*) was used (to varying degrees) throughout the lengthy transition between the two.

In [early Greek alphabets](/wiki/Epichoric_alphabets "Epichoric alphabets"), the shape and orientation of lambda varied.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Most variants consisted of two straight strokes, one longer than the other, connected at their ends. The angle might be in the upper-left, lower-left ("Western" alphabets) or top ("Eastern" alphabets). Other variants had a vertical line with a horizontal or sloped stroke running to the right. With the general adoption of the [Ionic alphabet](/wiki/History_of_the_Greek_alphabet#Standardization_–_the_Ionic_alphabet "History of the Greek alphabet"), Greek settled on an angle at the top; the Romans put the angle at the lower-left.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/NAMA_Alphabet_grec.jpg/250px-NAMA_Alphabet_grec.jpg)](/wiki/File:NAMA_Alphabet_grec.jpg)

The Greek alphabet on a [black figure](/wiki/Black_figure "Black figure") [vessel](/wiki/Bowl_(vessel) "Bowl (vessel)"), with a Phoenician-lamed-shaped lambda. The gamma has the shape of modern lambda.

## Symbol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lambda&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Symbol")]

### Upper-case letter Λ

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lambda&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Upper-case letter Λ")]

Examples of the symbolic use of uppercase lambda include:

* The [lambda particle](/wiki/Lambda_particle "Lambda particle") is a type of [subatomic particle](/wiki/Subatomic_particle "Subatomic particle") in [subatomic particle physics](/wiki/Physics#High_energy/particle_physics "Physics").
* Lambda is the set of logical axioms in the [axiomatic method](/wiki/Axiomatic_method "Axiomatic method") of logical deduction in [first-order logic](/wiki/First-order_logic "First-order logic").
* Uppercase Lambda is often used as a stylized form of uppercase [A](/wiki/A "A"), especially in science fiction.
* There is a poetical allusion to the use of Lambda as a [shield blazon](/wiki/Aspis "Aspis") by the [Spartans](/wiki/Spartans "Spartans").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[5]](#cite_note-5)
* Lambda is the [von Mangoldt function](/wiki/Von_Mangoldt_function "Von Mangoldt function") in mathematical [number theory](/wiki/Number_theory "Number theory").[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* Lambda denotes the [de Bruijn–Newman constant](/wiki/De_Bruijn%E2%80%93Newman_constant "De Bruijn–Newman constant") which is closely connected with [Riemann's hypothesis](/wiki/Riemann%27s_hypothesis "Riemann's hypothesis").[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* In [statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics"), lambda is used for the [likelihood ratio](/wiki/Likelihood_function#Likelihood_ratio "Likelihood function").
* In statistics, [Wilks's lambda](/wiki/Samuel_Stanley_Wilks "Samuel Stanley Wilks") is used in multivariate analysis of variance ([MANOVA](/wiki/MANOVA "MANOVA") analysis) to compare group means on a combination of dependent variables.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* In the [spectral decomposition of matrices](/wiki/Eigendecomposition_of_a_matrix#Normal_matrices "Eigendecomposition of a matrix"), lambda indicates the [diagonal matrix](/wiki/Diagonal_matrix "Diagonal matrix") of the [eigenvalues](/wiki/Eigenvalues "Eigenvalues") of the [matrix](/wiki/Matrix_(mathematics) "Matrix (mathematics)").
* In [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), lambda is the time window over which a process is observed for determining the working memory set for a [digital computer](/wiki/Digital_computer "Digital computer")'s [virtual memory](/wiki/Virtual_memory "Virtual memory") management.
* In [astrophysics](/wiki/Astrophysics "Astrophysics"), lambda represents the likelihood that a small body will encounter a [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet") or a [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet") leading to a deflection of a significant magnitude. An object with a large value of lambda is expected to have [cleared its neighbourhood](/wiki/Clearing_the_neighbourhood "Clearing the neighbourhood"), satisfying the current [definition of a planet](/wiki/Definition_of_planet "Definition of planet").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* In [crystal optics](/wiki/Crystal_optics "Crystal optics"), lambda is used to represent a lattice period.
* In [electrochemistry](/wiki/Electrochemistry "Electrochemistry"), lambda denotes the "equivalent [conductance](/wiki/Electrical_conductance "Electrical conductance")" of an [electrolyte](/wiki/Electrolyte "Electrolyte") solution.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* In [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology"), lambda is the symbol for the [cosmological constant](/wiki/Cosmological_constant "Cosmological constant"), a term added to some dynamical equations to account for the [accelerating expansion of the universe](/wiki/Accelerating_expansion_of_the_universe "Accelerating expansion of the universe").[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* In optics, lambda denotes the grating pitch of a [Bragg reflector](/wiki/Bragg_reflector "Bragg reflector").[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* In [politics](/wiki/Politics "Politics"), the lambda is the symbol of [Identitarianism](/wiki/Identitarian_movement "Identitarian movement"), a [white nationalist](/wiki/White_nationalist "White nationalist") movement that originated in [France](/wiki/France "France") before spreading out to the rest of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and later on to [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). The Identitarian lambda represents the [Battle of Thermopylae](/wiki/Battle_of_Thermopylae "Battle of Thermopylae").

### Lower-case letter λ

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lambda&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lower-case letter λ")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Greek_lc_lamda_thin.svg/250px-Greek_lc_lamda_thin.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Greek_lc_lamda_thin.svg)

Lower-case lambda

Examples of the symbolic use of lowercase lambda include:

* λ indicates the [wavelength](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength") of any [wave](/wiki/Wave "Wave"), especially in [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [electrical engineering](/wiki/Electrical_engineering "Electrical engineering"), and [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics").[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* In [evolutionary algorithms](/wiki/Evolutionary_algorithm "Evolutionary algorithm"), λ indicates the number of offspring that would be generated from μ current population in each generation. The terms μ and λ are originated from [evolution strategy](/wiki/Evolution_strategy "Evolution strategy") notation.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* λ indicates the [radioactivity decay constant](/wiki/Exponential_decay "Exponential decay") in [nuclear physics](/wiki/Nuclear_physics "Nuclear physics") and [radioactivity](/wiki/Radioactivity "Radioactivity"). This constant is very simply related (by a multiplicative constant) to the [half-life](/wiki/Half-life "Half-life") of any radioactive material.
* In [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"), λ represents the density of occurrences within a time interval, as modelled by the [Poisson distribution](/wiki/Poisson_distribution "Poisson distribution").[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* In [mathematical logic](/wiki/Mathematical_logic "Mathematical logic") and [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), λ is used to introduce [anonymous functions](/wiki/Anonymous_function "Anonymous function") expressed with the concepts of [lambda calculus](/wiki/Lambda_calculus "Lambda calculus").
* λ indicates an [eigenvalue](/wiki/Eigenvalue "Eigenvalue") in the mathematics of [linear algebra](/wiki/Linear_algebra "Linear algebra").
* In the physics of particles, lambda indicates the [thermal de Broglie wavelength](/wiki/Thermal_de_Broglie_wavelength "Thermal de Broglie wavelength")[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* In the physics of [electric fields](/wiki/Electric_field "Electric field"), lambda sometimes indicates the linear [charge density](/wiki/Charge_density "Charge density") of a uniform line of [electric charge](/wiki/Electric_charge "Electric charge") (measured in [coulombs](/wiki/Coulomb "Coulomb") per meter).
* Lambda denotes a [Lagrange multiplier](/wiki/Lagrange_multiplier "Lagrange multiplier") in [multi-dimensional calculus](/wiki/Multivariable_calculus "Multivariable calculus").[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* In [solid-state electronics](/wiki/Solid-state_electronics "Solid-state electronics"), lambda indicates the [channel length modulation](/wiki/Channel_length_modulation "Channel length modulation") parameter of a [MOSFET](/wiki/MOSFET "MOSFET").[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* In [ecology](/wiki/Ecology "Ecology"), lambda denotes the long-term intrinsic growth rate of a population.[[19]](#cite_note-19) This value is often calculated as the dominant eigenvalue of the age/size class [matrix](/wiki/Matrix_(mathematics) "Matrix (mathematics)").
* In [formal language theory](/wiki/Formal_language_theory "Formal language theory") and in computer science, lambda denotes the [empty string](/wiki/Empty_string "Empty string").[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* Lambda is a nonstandard symbol in the [International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Obsolete_and_nonstandard_symbols_in_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Obsolete and nonstandard symbols in the International Phonetic Alphabet") for the [voiced alveolar lateral affricate](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_lateral_affricate "Voiced alveolar lateral affricate") [[dɮ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_lateral_affricate "Voiced alveolar lateral affricate")].[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* Lambda denotes the [Lebesgue measure](/wiki/Lebesgue_measure "Lebesgue measure") in [mathematical set theory](/wiki/Set_theory "Set theory").[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* The [Goodman and Kruskal's lambda](/wiki/Goodman_and_Kruskal%27s_lambda "Goodman and Kruskal's lambda") in [statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics") indicates the [proportional reduction in error](/wiki/Proportional_reduction_in_error "Proportional reduction in error") when one variable's values are used to predict the values of another variable.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* Lambda denotes the [oxygen sensor](/wiki/Oxygen_sensor "Oxygen sensor") in a vehicle that measures the [air-to-fuel ratio](/wiki/Air-fuel_ratio "Air-fuel ratio") in the exhaust [gases](/wiki/Gases "Gases") of an [internal-combustion engine](/wiki/Internal-combustion_engine "Internal-combustion engine").[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* A [Lambda 4S](/wiki/Lambda_(rocket) "Lambda (rocket)") solid-fuel rocket was used to launch [Japan's first orbital satellite](/wiki/Ohsumi_(satellite) "Ohsumi (satellite)") in 1970.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* Lambda denotes the [failure rate](/wiki/Failure_rate "Failure rate") of devices and systems in [reliability theory](/wiki/Reliability_theory "Reliability theory"), and it is measured in failure events per hour. Numerically, this lambda is also the reciprocal of the [mean time between failures](/wiki/Mean_time_between_failure "Mean time between failure").[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* In criminology, lambda denotes an individual's frequency of [offences](/wiki/Offence_(law) "Offence (law)").[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* In [electrochemistry](/wiki/Electrochemistry "Electrochemistry"), lambda also denotes the [ionic](/wiki/Ion "Ion") conductance of a given ion (the composition of the ion is generally shown as a subscript to the lambda character).
* In [neurobiology](/wiki/Neurobiology "Neurobiology"), lambda denotes the [length constant](/wiki/Length_constant "Length constant") (or exponential rate of decay) of the [electric potential](/wiki/Electric_potential "Electric potential") across the [cell membrane](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane") along a length of a nerve cell's [axon](/wiki/Axon "Axon").
* In the science and technology of [heat transfer](/wiki/Heat_transfer "Heat transfer"), lambda denotes the [heat of vaporization](/wiki/Heat_of_vaporization "Heat of vaporization") per [mole](/wiki/Mole_(unit) "Mole (unit)") of material (a.k.a. its "latent heat").[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* In the technology and science of [celestial navigation](/wiki/Celestial_navigation "Celestial navigation"), lambda denotes the [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") as opposed to the Roman letter "L", which denotes the [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude").
* A block style lambda is used as a recurring symbol in the [Valve](/wiki/Valve_Corporation "Valve Corporation") computer game series *[Half-Life](/wiki/Half-Life_(series) "Half-Life (series)")*,[[29]](#cite_note-29) referring to the Lambda Complex of the fictional [Black Mesa Research Facility](/wiki/Black_Mesa_Research_Facility "Black Mesa Research Facility"), as well as making appearances in the sequel *[Half-Life 2](/wiki/Half-Life_2 "Half-Life 2")*, and its subsequent prequel *[Half-Life: Alyx](/wiki/Half-Life:_Alyx "Half-Life: Alyx")* as an in-universe [symbol of resistance](/wiki/Political_symbolism "Political symbolism").[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* In 1970, a lowercase lambda was chosen by [Tom Doerr](/wiki/Tom_Doerr "Tom Doerr") as the symbol of the New York chapter of the [Gay Activists Alliance](/wiki/Gay_Activists_Alliance "Gay Activists Alliance").[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The lambda symbol became associated with [gay liberation](/wiki/Gay_liberation "Gay liberation")[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) and recognized as an [LGBTQ symbol](/wiki/LGBTQ_symbols "LGBTQ symbols") for some time afterwards, being used as such by the International Gay Rights Congress in Edinburgh.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* [Golomb–Dickman constant](/wiki/Golomb%E2%80%93Dickman_constant "Golomb–Dickman constant")[[36]](#cite_note-36)
* In continuum mechanics, lambda represents one of the [Lamé parameters](/wiki/Lam%C3%A9_parameters "Lamé parameters"), which is a material property that arises in stress-strain relationships.

### Litra symbol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lambda&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Litra symbol")]

The [Roman](/wiki/Roman_units "Roman units") *libra* and [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_units "Byzantine units") *lítra* (λίτρα), which served as both the pound mass unit and liter volume unit, were abbreviated in Greek using lambda with modified forms of the [iota subscript](/wiki/Iota_subscript "Iota subscript") ⟨λͅ⟩. These are variously encoded in Unicode. The [Ancient Greek Numbers](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_Numbers_(Unicode_block) "Ancient Greek Numbers (Unicode block)") [Unicode block](/wiki/Unicode_block "Unicode block") includes 10183 greek litra sign (𐆃) as well as 𐅢, which is described as 10162 greek acrophonic hermionian ten[[37]](#cite_note-37) but was much more common as a form of the litra sign. A variant of the sign can be formed from 0338 combining long solidus overlay and either 039B greek capital letter lamda (Λ̸) or 03BB greek small letter lamda (λ̸).[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Unicode

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lambda&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Unicode")]

Unicode uses the (Modern Greek-based) spelling "lamda" in character names, instead of "lambda", due to "the pre-existing names in ISO 8859-7, as well as preferences expressed by the Greek National Body".[[39]](#cite_note-39) Latin versions of lambda were added to Unicode in 2024 for the [Salishan](/wiki/Salishan_languages "Salishan languages") and [Wakashan languages](/wiki/Wakashan_languages "Wakashan languages") in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

* U+039B Λ GREEK CAPITAL LETTER LAMDA (Λ)[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* U+03BB λ GREEK SMALL LETTER LAMDA (λ)
* U+1D27 ᴧ GREEK LETTER SMALL CAPITAL LAMDA
* U+2C96 Ⲗ COPTIC CAPITAL LETTER LAULA
* U+2C97 ⲗ COPTIC SMALL LETTER LAULA
* U+A7DA Ꟛ LATIN CAPITAL LETTER LAMBDA
* U+A7DB ꟛ LATIN SMALL LETTER LAMBDA
* U+1038D 𐎍 UGARITIC LETTER LAMDA
* U+1D6B2 𝚲 MATHEMATICAL BOLD CAPITAL LAMDA[[a]](#cite_note-42)
* U+1D6CC 𝛌 MATHEMATICAL BOLD SMALL LAMDA
* U+1D6EC 𝛬 MATHEMATICAL ITALIC CAPITAL LAMDA
* U+1D706 𝜆 MATHEMATICAL ITALIC SMALL LAMDA
* U+1D726 𝜦 MATHEMATICAL BOLD ITALIC CAPITAL LAMDA
* U+1D740 𝝀 MATHEMATICAL BOLD ITALIC SMALL LAMDA
* U+1D760 𝝠 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD CAPITAL LAMDA
* U+1D77A 𝝺 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD SMALL LAMDA
* U+1D79A 𝞚 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD ITALIC CAPITAL LAMDA
* U+1D7B4 𝞴 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD ITALIC SMALL LAMDA

1. **[^](#cite_ref-42)** The mathematical characters should only be used in math. Stylized Greek text should be encoded using the normal Greek letters, with markup and formatting to indicate text style.
